name: Israel Shobowale
title: AWS Cloud Support Engineer
tagline: Cloud engineer focused on secure, well-documented deployments. I build and maintain cloud infrastructure, monitoring performance and resolving issues before they become outages.
summary: I got into cloud engineering because I like taking things apart to see how they actually work. Most of what I do comes down to one question: what happens when this breaks? I build cloud infrastructure with that in mind. Secure and properly documented. Currently deep in AWS and Azure, chasing the AWS SAA cert, and using my own portfolio site as a testbed for the CI/CD pipelines I'm learning to build.

experience.summary: Day-to-day cloud support for a system storing sensitive client documentation and support records.
highlights: Monitor IAM access across the organisation's cloud environment, reviewing and restricting permissions to enforce least-privilege for staff and systems.
Review CloudWatch logs daily for the application hosting client documentation and support records, checking for anomalies and access issues.
Support operational continuity of a system storing sensitive client data, applying consistent monitoring discipline.

experience.summary: Hands-on AWS project work — deployment, infrastructure-as-code, and live-fault troubleshooting.
highlights: Deployed and configured applications on AWS EC2 using CloudFormation, integrating S3 for media storage.
Diagnosed a live application fault (broken image loading) via CloudWatch logs, traced it to a misconfigured CloudFormation resource, corrected it and redeployed.
Administered infrastructure through AWS Systems Manager Session Manager, using least-privilege access in place of direct SSH.
Applied EC2, S3, CloudWatch, IaC (CloudFormation) and IAM hands-on through project work rather than theory alone.

writeup.text: My portfolio site, plus the AWS setup that builds, deploys and monitors it.

This site is the project. It's plain HTML, CSS and JavaScript with no framework, written with AI assistance and deployed on AWS. I designed the architecture, built and configured everything on the AWS side, and I maintain it. The diagram below shows the whole system; the notes are on the decisions worth explaining.

The code lives in GitHub. Amplify watches the repo and rebuilds on every push to main, and handles hosting, the CDN and the TLS certificate. Route 53 provides DNS for israelondemand.dev, a .dev domain, which browsers refuse to load without HTTPS, so that had to work from the start.
Why Amplify and not S3 with CloudFront

I built it the manual way first: S3 for the files, CloudFront for HTTPS and caching, GitHub Actions deploying on push through an IAM role assumed with OIDC, so no keys were stored in the repo. The workflow ran and authenticated, but I kept hitting failures on the role's trust policy, and chose to ship on Amplify rather than keep debugging. Amplify does in one service what those four did between them. I traded fine control over caching for a working site with far less to maintain the right call at this size. I still plan to finish the S3 and CloudFront version as its own exercise.

The visit counter is a small serverless API: the browser calls a Lambda through a Function URL, and it increments two counters in DynamoDB, an all-time total and one per day. The increment uses DynamoDB's atomic ADD rather than reading and writing back, so two visitors at the same instant can't overwrite each other. The Lambda's role can call UpdateItem on that one table and nothing else, and the counter stores no IPs, location or cookies. If the API doesn't respond, the counter removes itself rather than showing a broken sidebar.

Two alarms share one path: a CloudWatch alarm on Lambda errors and an AWS Budgets threshold on spend both email me through a single SNS topic, so I hear early if the counter breaks or cost creeps up. Log retention is set to 30 days. Nothing runs on a server and there's no VPC the only real monthly cost is the Route 53 hosted zone.

projects.title: Tic-tac-toe, playable here
projects.blurb: Feeling Up to it? play tic tac toe with ISRL, warning, I can never loose. You can also play with a friend so you get a fighting chance. 😉

writeup.text: A small X & O game built in plain JavaScript no framework, no build step. Play it above: switch between two-player and the computer opponent ISRL, which SERIOUSLY cannot be beaten.
It ships as part of this site — static files in GitHub, deployed automatically to AWS Amplify on every push, with no server or database behind it.
